answer:
In the Urdu corpus—primarily poetic works such as *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, *Diwan-e-Zauq*, and Allama Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara* (Parts 1–3)—**fitna** (فِتنَہ) is a recurring motif denoting mischief, sedition, trial, or disruptive temptation.[^1] It often evokes a seductive chaos or alluring danger, especially tied to love (*ishq*), beauty, longing (*intezaar*), or existential upheaval, aligning with classical Persianate poetic traditions where the beloved's charms sow discord akin to a trial or cosmic strife.[^2]

### Key Contexts and Usages
- **Personified as a mischievous agent (*fitna-gar*)**: Love or a glance is depicted as cunning and rebellious, capable of piercing heavens or causing rebellion. In Iqbal, love is "fitna-gar-o-sarkash-o-chaalaak" (mischievous, rebellious, cunning).[^3] Zauq similarly calls someone a *fitna-gar* seen in dreams, evoking dread like Judgment Day at dawn.[^4]
- **Linked to longing or waiting (*fitna-e-intezaar*)**: Ghalib describes himself (via pen-name Asad) as "Asad-e-fitna-e-intezaar" (lion of waiting's mischief), burning in every color like a moth to love's flame.[^5]
- **As a disruptive gaze or beauty (*fitna-khiraam*)**: Eyes or glances "fitna-khiraam o dar-do-aalam baaz" (sowing discord, opening two worlds' doors), blending temptation with ruin.[^6] Beauty's trial desolates the heart: "Yeh fitna aadmi ki khaana-weerani ko kya kam hai" (this mischief—isn't man's hearth-desolation enough?).[^7]
- **Doomsday or cosmic chaos (*fitna-e-mahshar*, *fitna-khoo*)**: Ghalib uses it for apocalyptic mischief ("mo'taqid-e-fitna-e-mahshar," believer in doomsday's discord; "fitna-khoo," mischief-devourer) or intoxicating ruin ("mast-e-fitna-khu").[^8] It appears as an "essence" (*arq-e-fitna*) or strength-stealing force (*fitna-e-taaqat-ruba*.[^9]
- **Absence of discord (*fitna-o-fasaad*)**: In assemblies or life, its lack signals dullness: "aaj bazm mein kuchh fitna-o-fasaad nahin" (today no mischief-strife in the gathering).[^10]

No prose treatises define *fitna* philosophically; all instances are in ghazals/nazms, where it symbolizes the perilous allure testing the lover's soul, echoing Qur'anic trials but stylized as romantic peril. It appears ~15 times, mostly in Ghalib (10+), underscoring his mastery of paradox.

[^1]: Aggregated from *search_corpus* hits across corpus; e.g., *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, ch. 4, 25, 68, etc.
[^2]: *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, ch. 4 (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/04-aaina-dekh-apna-sa-munh-le-ke-reh-gaye/transliteration/).
[^3]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4 (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/transliteration/#p-aaae7c).
[^4]: *Diwan-e-Zauq*, ch. 17 (/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/17-gauhar-ko-jauhari-sarraf-zar-ko-dekhte-hain/transliteration/).
[^5]: *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, ch. 4 (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/04-aaina-dekh-apna-sa-munh-le-ke-reh-gaye/transliteration/).
[^6]: *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, ch. 68 (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/68-hareef-e-matlab-e-mushkil-nahin-fusoon-e-niyaaz/transliteration/).
[^7]: *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, ch. 112 (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/112-kisi-ko-de-ke-dil-koi-nawasanj-e-fughan-kyun-ho/transliteration/).
[^8]: *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, chs. 192, 197 (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/192-tu-dost-kisu-ka-bhi-sitam-gar-na-hua-tha/transliteration/), (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/197-warasta-uss-se-hain-ke-mohabbat-hi-kyun-na-ho/transliteration/).
[^9]: *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, chs. 150, 220 (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/150-nafas-na-anjuman-e-arzoo-se-baahar-khainch/transliteration/), (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/220-hawas-ko-hai-nishaat-e-kaar-kya-kya/transliteration/).
[^10]: *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, ch. 151 (/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/151-nahin-ke-mujh-ko-qayamat-ka-aiteqad-nahin/transliteration/).
